s: Perovskite crystals can convert sunlight to electricity more efficiently than silicon at a fraction of the cost, potentially revolutionizing solar energy.
q: What crystal material could replace silicon in solar panels?
e: Perovskite solar cells have reached over 33% efficiency in lab settings and can be printed like newspaper, making them far cheaper to manufacture.

s: The archerfish can shoot a jet of water from its mouth to knock insects off branches above the water with remarkable accuracy from up to 3 meters away.
q: How does the archerfish catch its prey?
a: By shooting water jets to knock insects from branches
d: By using its teeth to grab insects
By creating whirlpools to trap food
By jumping out of the water
By using its fins to knock insects down
e: Archerfish compensate for light refraction at the water-air interface, adjusting their aim to account for how objects appear shifted from below.

s: Muon particles from cosmic rays pass through your body at a rate of about 10,000 per minute — they travel at nearly the speed of light.
q: About how many cosmic ray muons pass through your body per minute?
e: Muons are created when cosmic rays hit Earth's atmosphere, and due to relativistic time dilation, they survive long enough to reach the ground.

s: The Sahara Desert was green and lush with lakes and rivers just 5,000-10,000 years ago during the 'African Humid Period.'
q: When was the Sahara Desert last green and lush?
e: Changes in Earth's orbital parameters increased monsoon rainfall, turning the Sahara into savanna with hippopotamus-filled lakes and human settlements.

s: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature — it freezes at -38.83°C, far below any normal environment.
q: What is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature?
e: Mercury's electron configuration results in unusually weak metallic bonds between atoms, keeping it liquid at everyday temperatures.

s: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards, upside down, and hover in place — their wings beat up to 80 times per second.
q: How many times per second can a hummingbird's wings beat?
e: Their unique ball-and-socket shoulder joint allows 180-degree rotation, generating lift on both the upstroke and downstroke.

s: The longest known cave system in the world is Mammoth Cave in Kentucky at over 680 km (420 miles) of surveyed passageways — and it's still being explored.
q: What is the longest known cave system in the world?
e: New passages are discovered regularly, and Mammoth Cave may be connected to nearby cave systems, potentially making it even longer.

s: Some deep-sea fish have transparent heads — the barreleye fish (Macropinna microstoma) has a see-through dome skull revealing its tubular eyes pointing upward.
q: What is unusual about the barreleye fish's head?
a: It has a transparent dome skull
d: It has a very large brain
e: The transparent head allows the barreleye to look upward through its skull for prey silhouettes against faint surface light in the deep ocean.

s: Gallium metal melts in your hand — its melting point is only 29.76°C (85.6°F), just above room temperature.
q: What is the approximate melting point of gallium?
e: Gallium looks like aluminum but becomes a liquid puddle when held, and it's safe to handle unlike mercury.

s: Ants have been farming fungi for about 50 million years — leafcutter ants cultivate specific fungus gardens as their primary food source.
q: What do leafcutter ants grow as their primary food source?
e: Leafcutter ants don't eat the leaves they cut; they use them as compost to grow a specialized fungus that exists nowhere else in nature.

s: The smell after rain — called petrichor — is caused by geosmin, a chemical produced by soil bacteria (Streptomyces) that humans can detect at just 5 parts per trillion.
q: What is the name for the smell after rain?
e: Raindrops hitting soil release aerosols containing geosmin, and humans are 200,000 times more sensitive to this chemical than sharks are to blood.

s: Black holes aren't entirely black — Stephen Hawking predicted they slowly emit radiation (Hawking radiation) and will eventually evaporate over unimaginably long timescales.
q: What is the radiation slowly emitted by black holes called?
e: Quantum effects near the event horizon allow particle-antiparticle pairs to form, with one escaping and the other falling in, causing the black hole to lose mass.

s: A bolt of lightning is five times hotter than the surface of the Sun
q: How much hotter is lightning than the Sun's surface?
e: Lightning superheats the narrow air channel it travels through almost instantaneously. The Sun's photosphere is hot, but lightning's extreme compression and electrical discharge briefly exceeds it by a large margin.

s: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe
q: More possible chess games or atoms in universe?
d: piece(s) of firewood (esp. chopped or split from logs)
e: Claude Shannon estimated this in 1950. Even though chess has simple rules, the branching factor at each move creates astronomical combinations. This is why computers still cannot solve chess completely by brute force.

s: Neptune has winds that travel faster than the speed of sound on Earth
q: How fast do Neptune's winds travel?
e: The speed of sound in Earth's atmosphere at sea level is about 1,235 km/h. Neptune's winds, despite it being the coldest major planet, are driven by internal heat and atmospheric dynamics that scientists still do not fully understand.

s: It rains diamonds on Neptune and Uranus
q: What unusual precipitation occurs inside Neptune and Uranus?
e: Scientists confirmed this through laboratory experiments replicating the extreme pressures inside ice giants. Temperatures of thousands of degrees and pressures millions of times greater than Earth's atmosphere cause carbon atoms to crystallise into diamonds.

s: A neutron star is so dense that a teaspoon of its material would weigh about a billion tonnes on Earth
q: How much would a teaspoon of neutron star material weigh?
e: Neutron stars form when massive stars collapse. The gravity is so extreme that protons and electrons are forced to merge into neutrons, packed as densely as an atomic nucleus. A typical neutron star is 20 km across but contains more mass than the Sun.

s: Oxygen was not always a major component of Earth's atmosphere — it was once almost nonexistent
q: What caused Earth's atmosphere to become oxygen-rich?
e: Early Earth's atmosphere was rich in nitrogen, methane, and carbon dioxide. Cyanobacteria evolved oxygenic photosynthesis and began releasing oxygen as waste. To anaerobic life forms, this oxygen was toxic — it caused a mass extinction before complex life could even begin.

s: Hot water can freeze faster than cold water under certain conditions — this is called the Mpemba effect
q: What is the Mpemba effect?
a: Hot water freezes faster than cold
e: The Mpemba effect is real but not universal. Proposed explanations include evaporation reducing mass, dissolved gases escaping, convection patterns, and hydrogen bond dynamics. It works reliably only under specific conditions, which is why it remained controversial for decades.

s: The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is now in interstellar space — the first human-made object to leave the solar system
q: When did Voyager 1 enter interstellar space?
e: Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause — the boundary where the Sun's solar wind is overcome by interstellar medium — in 2012. It carries a Golden Record with sounds and images of Earth, intended as a message to any extraterrestrial intelligence that might find it.

s: Sound cannot travel through space — space is a near-perfect vacuum
q: Why can't sound travel through outer space?
a: Space lacks a medium for sound waves
e: Sound is a mechanical wave that requires a medium — particles to compress and rarefy. The vacuum of space has far too few particles per cubic metre to transmit any sound wave at audible frequencies. Gas clouds in space can carry very low-frequency pressure waves, but nothing a human ear can detect.

s: The Sahara Desert was a lush, green savanna with lakes and rivers only 6,000 years ago
q: What was the Sahara Desert like 6,000 years ago?
e: The African Humid Period, also called the Green Sahara, occurred due to shifts in Earth's orbital cycle (Milankovitch cycles) that brought monsoon rains deeper into Africa. The transition back to desert happened rapidly — possibly within centuries — due to vegetation feedbacks.

s: Water expands by about 9% when it freezes — which is why ice floats and pipes burst in winter
q: By approximately how much does water expand when freezing?
e: When water molecules arrange into ice crystals, they form a hexagonal lattice with more empty space between molecules than liquid water. This lower density makes ice float. Without this anomaly, lakes would freeze solid from the bottom up, wiping out aquatic life every winter.

s: The centre of the Earth is approximately the same temperature as the surface of the Sun
q: Earth's core temperature compares to Sun's surface how?
e: Earth's inner core reaches temperatures between 5,000°C and 6,000°C, maintained by residual heat from planetary formation and ongoing radioactive decay of uranium, thorium, and potassium. Despite the extreme heat, the inner core is solid due to immense pressure.

s: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus
q: Why is a Venus day longer than its year?
e: Venus's extremely slow retrograde rotation is thought to result from a collision early in solar system history or tidal locking interactions. Its thick atmosphere also rotates much faster than the planet itself, creating winds of up to 350 km/h.

s: Helium was discovered on the Sun before it was found on Earth
q: Where was helium first discovered?
e: Pierre Janssen and Norman Lockyer independently detected helium's spectral signature during a solar eclipse in 1868. Helium was isolated on Earth in 1895 by William Ramsay from uranium ore (cleveite), where radioactive decay produces helium as alpha particles.

s: Quantum entanglement allows two particles to be instantly correlated regardless of the distance between them
q: What did Einstein call quantum entanglement?
e: Entangled particles share a quantum state. When you measure one, you instantly know the state of the other — even across the universe. This does not allow faster-than-light communication because the outcome is random; you cannot control what result you get to send a message.

s: Glass is technically a supercooled liquid, not a solid — old window panes are thicker at the bottom because glass flows extremely slowly
q: Why are old medieval window panes thicker at bottom?
e: Medieval glass was made by spinning molten glass into discs, which were thicker at the edges — so glaziers placed the thicker edge downward for stability. True glass flow at room temperature requires geological timescales. The liquid-glass distinction is real but irrelevant to window thickness.

s: The human body contains enough carbon to make nearly 9,000 pencils
q: How much carbon does average adult contain?
e: Carbon is the backbone of all organic molecules — DNA, proteins, fats, carbohydrates. The carbon in your body was forged in the cores of ancient stars that exploded as supernovae billions of years ago, seeding space with the elements of life.

s: The observable universe is about 93 billion light-years in diameter, even though it is only 13.8 billion years old
q: What is the observable universe's approximate diameter?
e: The universe's expansion means distant objects moved away while their light was travelling. Using the comoving distance — accounting for expansion — the observable universe's diameter is about 93 billion light-years. This is not contradicted by relativity because it is space itself expanding, not objects moving through space.

s: The shortest unit of time ever measured is the zeptosecond — about 247 zeptoseconds for light to cross a hydrogen molecule
q: What is a zeptosecond in seconds?
e: Researchers at Goethe University Frankfurt used attosecond X-ray pulses to measure the photoionization process in hydrogen molecules. The 247 zeptosecond measurement represents the quantum mechanical traversal time of light across two atoms — approximately 0.000000000000000000000247 seconds.

s: Rainbows are actually full circles — we only see arcs because the ground cuts them off
q: What is a rainbow's complete geometric shape?
e: A rainbow forms at a specific angle (42°) from the antisolar point — the point directly opposite the Sun from your perspective. The full geometry is circular, but the ground intercepts the lower half. Pilots and people on tall mountains or above waterfalls sometimes see complete circles.

s: The admittance of vacuum is the reciprocal of the impedance of free space, approximately 1/377 siemens.
q: What is the approximate impedance of free space?
e: The admittance of vacuum describes how easily electromagnetic waves propagate through empty space. Its reciprocal, the impedance of free space (~377 ohms), determines how electric and magnetic fields relate in an electromagnetic wave. This value arises from the fundamental ratio of the permittivity and permeability of free space.

s: Agfacolor, the first practical color film for amateurs, was invented in Nazi Germany in 1936 — and after World War II, the Allies seized its formula as war reparations, seeding color film industries worldwide.
q: What happened to the Agfacolor film formula after World War II?
a: The Allies seized it as war reparations
d: The Allies shared it equally among all nations
It was lost to the Soviets
e: The Agfa company developed a subtractive color process using dye couplers incorporated directly into the film emulsion layers. After Germany's surrender, the formula was declared public domain and used by companies including Fujifilm (Japan), Ferrania (Italy), and Soviet film manufacturers.

s: The first widely used photographic printing process used egg whites (albumen) — at the peak of the albumen print era in the 1860s, photographers consumed an estimated 6 million eggs per year in Britain alone.
q: What unexpected ingredient powered the most popular photo printing process of the 1860s?
e: Albumen prints dominated photography from 1855 to about 1890. Egg whites were whipped, mixed with salt, spread on paper, and dried. The paper was then sensitized with silver nitrate to create a photographic surface with remarkable detail.

s: Aluminium is a silvery metal with symbol Al and atomic number 13.
q: What is aluminium's atomic number?
e: This fact highlights an important aspect of the topic worth remembering.

s: The Amazon rainforest is fertilized by dust blown all the way from the Sahara Desert — about 22,000 tons of phosphorus-rich dust crosses the Atlantic Ocean every year, keeping the jungle alive.
q: Where does the Amazon rainforest get much of its essential phosphorus?
a: From Sahara Desert dust blown across the Atlantic
d: From volcanic ash in the Andes
e: NASA's CALIPSO satellite tracked Saharan dust clouds crossing the Atlantic. The phosphorus in this dust replaces what gets washed away by Amazonian rainfall and river runoff. Much of the dust comes from the Bodélé Depression in Chad, which was once a lake bed. Without this transcontinental nutrient delivery, the Amazon would slowly become depleted.

s: Angelica archangelica is a medicinal herb used in traditional remedies.
q: What type of plant is Angelica archangelica?
e: Legend says an angel revealed angelica's healing powers to a monk!.

s: The total biomass of all ants on Earth — estimated at 80 million tons of dry carbon — exceeds the combined biomass of all wild birds and wild mammals put together.
q: How does the total biomass of all ants compare to that of all wild mammals?
a: Ants outweigh all wild mammals combined
d: Ants outweigh all wild birds combined
Ants weigh less than all wild mammals
Ants weigh less than all birds and mammals
Ants weigh more than all humans
e: A 2022 study in PNAS estimated there are approximately 20 quadrillion (20 x 10^15) individual ants on Earth, with a collective dry biomass of about 80 million tonnes of carbon. For comparison, wild mammals total about 60 million tonnes and wild birds about 2 million tonnes. Ants have been ecologically dominant for over 100 million years.

s: Antibubbles are the opposite of soap bubbles — instead of a thin film of liquid surrounding air, they are a thin shell of air surrounding a droplet of liquid, and they sink instead of float.
q: What is an antibubble?
a: A thin shell of air surrounding a liquid droplet that sinks
d: A bubble made of solid material
e: Antibubbles form when a droplet of soapy water falls into a pool of the same liquid. A thin air layer gets trapped around the droplet, creating a sphere that looks like a bubble but sinks because the liquid core is denser than air. They last only seconds before the air film ruptures.

s: Leafcutter ants do not eat the leaves they carry — they use them to farm fungus. These ants have been cultivating underground fungus gardens for 60 million years, making them the oldest farmers on Earth, predating human agriculture by 59.99 million years.
q: What do leafcutter ants actually do with the leaves they carry?
a: Use them to grow fungus gardens that they eat
e: Leafcutter ants chew leaves into mulch, fertilize it with their feces, and grow a specific fungus (Leucoagaricus gongylophorus) that they eat. They use antimicrobial bacteria on their bodies as pesticides and weed out competing mold — a complete agricultural system parallel to human farming.

s: The axolotl can regrow its brain — it's one of the only vertebrates that can regenerate not just limbs and organs, but actual brain tissue with full neural connectivity.
q: Which organ can the axolotl uniquely regenerate among vertebrates?
e: Unlike most animals, axolotls can regenerate complete limbs, their spinal cord, heart tissue, and parts of their brain. Scientists have found that their immune system plays a crucial role: macrophages at wound sites prevent scarring and instead signal stem cells to rebuild the missing tissue perfectly.

s: Some bacteria generate electricity — Geobacter species transfer electrons through tiny conductive protein filaments called 'nanowires,' and scientists are engineering them to create living batteries and bioelectronic devices.
q: How do Geobacter bacteria transfer electrons?
a: Through conductive protein filaments called nanowires
e: Geobacter metallireducens was discovered in 1987 in river sediment. These bacteria 'breathe' iron and manganese oxides instead of oxygen, transferring electrons through protein nanowires up to 100 micrometers long. Researchers have built microbial fuel cells that generate electricity from wastewater using these organisms.

s: There are more bacteria in your mouth right now than there are people who have ever lived on Earth — your oral microbiome alone contains 20 billion bacteria, replenished every few hours.
q: How many bacteria live in a human mouth?
e: The human mouth harbors over 700 species of bacteria. Despite brushing and mouthwash, bacterial populations recover within hours because they double every 4-12 hours. The entire human body hosts about 38 trillion bacteria — slightly more than the 30 trillion human cells.

s: Banana leaves are used as natural plates, cooking wrappers, and packaging across tropical cultures, serving over 500 million people daily.
q: What makes banana leaves ideal as natural plates?
a: They are waterproof, flexible, and large
d: They prevent spoilage by absorbing toxins
They remain rigid at high heat
e: Banana leaves are waterproof, flexible, and large enough to serve as plates and food wrappers across South and Southeast Asia, Africa, and Latin America. They add a subtle flavor when used to wrap food for steaming or grilling. Unlike paper or plastic, banana leaves are fully biodegradable and require no energy to manufacture.

s: Bananas are naturally radioactive — they contain potassium-40, and scientists even created the 'banana equivalent dose' as an informal unit to help explain radiation exposure to the public.
q: What radioactive isotope makes bananas naturally radioactive?
e: A single banana contains about 15 becquerels of radioactivity from potassium-40. While harmless (your body regulates potassium levels regardless of intake), the banana equivalent dose (BED) helps contextualize radiation: a dental X-ray is about 50 bananas, and living near a nuclear plant for a year is about 1 banana.

s: Barium is a chemical element with symbol Ba and atomic number 56, a soft alkaline earth metal that reacts vigorously with water.
q: What color does barium produce in fireworks?
e: Barium compounds produce a bright green color in fireworks and signal flares. Barium sulfate is used in medical imaging as a contrast agent for X-rays of the digestive system. The element was first isolated by Sir Humphry Davy in 1808 through electrolysis.

s: Bark is the outer protective tissue covering tree trunks and branches.
q: What part of a tree does bark protect?
e: This fact highlights an important aspect of the topic worth remembering.

s: Beryllium is a lightweight element with symbol Be and atomic number 4.
q: What is beryllium's chemical symbol?
e: Beryllium is one-third the weight of aluminum but very stiff!.

s: About 76% of all ocean creatures produce their own light — bioluminescence is the most common form of communication on Earth, yet we rarely see it because most of it happens in the deep ocean.
q: What percentage of ocean creatures are estimated to produce bioluminescence?
e: A 2017 MBARI study found that three-quarters of marine organisms in Monterey Bay from surface to 4,000 meters depth can produce light. They use it for hunting (anglerfish lures), defense (ink squid release luminous clouds), communication (firefly squid), and camouflage (counter-illumination). Most bioluminescence uses the enzyme luciferase to oxidize luciferin.
